summary: Iranian FM Araghchi confirmed a 14-point MoU framework on June 13. Terms include: halt to fighting on all fronts including Lebanon, lifting of US naval blockade, reopening Strait of Hormuz under Iranian arrangements within 30 days, release of $24B frozen assets, 60-day nuclear negotiation window, US non-interference pledge, suspension of oil and petrochemical sanctions.

Iranian Foreign Ministry stated on June 14 that no signing date is 'established and confirmed,' contradicting Trump's claim that the MoU would be signed 'Sunday.' Pakistani PM Sharif stated the deal was 'finalized' and readied an electronic signing event. ABCP composite signing probability within 72 hours: 0.78.

Khamenei issued a directive barring uranium transfer abroad three days before the MoU's planned announcement. A US official simultaneously claimed Iran 'will agree in principle to dispose' of the 440.9 kg stockpile of 60%-enriched uranium. Structural incompatibility: Khamenei red line vs. US disposal demand is the primary fault line in Stage 2 nuclear talks.

IAEA confirmed Iran holds 440.9 kg of uranium enriched to 60% purity — one technical step below weapons-grade. US proposal requires halt to enrichment for minimum 12 years and surrender of stockpile. Iran's consistent position: enrichment is an NPT-guaranteed right; enriched material will not be exported.

Israel conducted strikes on southern Beirut on June 14 despite Iran insisting Lebanon is covered by the MoU. Lebanon ceasefire (extended 45 days from April 16) is formally separate from the US-Iran MoU but Iran has made Lebanon cessation a precondition. 61% of Israelis oppose US-Iran ceasefire; 73% expect hostilities to resume within a year.

Trump announced June 2 that Israel and Hezbollah reached a mutual cessation agreement. Hezbollah MP Hassan Fadlallah backed a 'full ceasefire on all Lebanese territory.' Israeli military operations have continued, citing Hezbollah projectile launches as ceasefire violations. Sequencing trap: Iran demands visible Israeli restraint before MoU finalization.

Iranian state media via IRNA confirmed missile program is explicitly excluded from the 60-day nuclear talks. Only three issues will be addressed in follow-on talks. This represents a significant US concession from earlier negotiating positions that demanded missile program inclusion.

Strait of Hormuz closure described by IEA as 'largest supply disruption in history of global oil market.' Wood Mackenzie: Gulf crude curtailed >11M bpd; worst-case scenario $200/bbl. Brent peaked ~$119/bbl in late April; fell to ~$94-98 on deal optimism in late May-early June. US SPR near 40-year low. UAE exited OPEC+.

Ukraine Commander-in-Chief Syrskyi confirmed 600+ sq km recaptured in first five months of 2026. ISW assessed Russia net territorial loss of approximately 281 sq km in May vs. ~40 sq km gained. Russian advance rate collapsed from 9.76 sq km/day in Q1 2025 to 2.9 sq km/day in Q1 2026 — deceleration of over two-thirds.

Ukraine drone campaign targeted 180,000 objectives in May — 12.7% increase over April. June 13: Ukrainian drones damaged Volna maritime oil terminal in Krasnodar's Temryuk district. June 12: Strikes on Volgograd oil processing facility. May 30: Tu-142 bombers destroyed at Taganrog airbase. Ilsky and Novoshakhtinsky refineries struck. Crimea fuel rationing in effect; most Sevastopol fuel stations dry.

Brigadier Biletsky stated in May that Russia is 'worn out and unable to achieve significant breakthroughs' and identified a critical six-month window for Ukraine to seize battlefield initiative ahead of potential peace talks. Also attributed Russian communication degradation to Starlink restrictions by Musk — variable outside Ukrainian control.

Zelenskyy invited Putin to direct talks and proposed a 'full ceasefire.' Putin declined — 'no reason for such a meeting at this time.' UK, France, and Germany (E3) backed Zelenskyy's peace call. EU envoys agreed to first-phase membership talks for Ukraine and Moldova.

Delcy Rodríguez governs as US-supervised interim president (sworn in January 5). Trump lifted sanctions April 1; Secretary Rubio confirmed compliance with US demands. US State Department resumed Caracas embassy operations. Venezuela oil revenue channeled through restricted Qatar account requiring US authorization. First transaction: 30-50M barrels transferred to US, $500M revenue to Venezuela.

Maduro and Cilia Flores appeared in US District Court (SDNY, Judge Hellerstein) on March 26 seeking dismissal on sovereignty grounds. Charges: narco-terrorism, cocaine importation, weapons. Next hearing: June 30, 2026. Amnesty law (February 20) released 448+ political detainees; 600+ estimated remaining.

Rodríguez replaced Defense Minister Padrino with General Gustavo González López on March 18, consolidating her position and signaling departure from Maduro-era security apparatus. IMF and World Bank resumed formal relations with Venezuela. Opposition (Machado faction) boycotted the May-elected National Assembly — legitimacy deficit persists.

Reuters/Ipsos June 3-8 poll: Trump approval 35%, disapproval 63%, net -28%. Consistent with May 15-18 reading (35/63). April 24-27: 34/64. Pollster average June 4: 38.3% approve, 58.5% disapprove. Only 22% approve of Trump's handling of living costs; 70% disapprove. Approval among independents and Hispanics tracking ~28-29%.

Generic congressional ballot June 13: Democrats +5.8% (41.9% vs 36.1%). New national poll June 12 shows Democrats holding double-digit lead. Structural models project Republican House losses of ~11-12 seats — would flip chamber. Democrats need net +4 to reclaim majority. Virginia redistricting approved, favorable to Democrats.

Thomas Massie lost Kentucky Republican primary to a Trump-backed Navy SEAL candidate. Massie was one of the few GOP members willing to challenge Trump. Loss confirms Trump's disciplinary grip on House Republicans heading into midterms; reduces intra-caucus resistance to executive overreach.

Netanyahu corruption trial resumed April 9 after state of emergency lifted. Netanyahu has repeatedly sought delays citing 'sensitive security and diplomatic factors.' Trump called for cancellation of Netanyahu's corruption trial in June 2025 and attacked Israeli prosecutors, linking US aid to trial outcome. President Herzog mediating for plea deal. Israeli elections scheduled before October 27.

Iran oil revenue loss estimated at ~$6B from Hormuz closure (Al Jazeera, June 5). OPEC+ announced 188,000 bpd output increase for June. Zarif's April 2026 regional enrichment consortium framework — proposing regional fuel-enrichment under international oversight — remains the most viable long-term nuclear bridge but is not in the current MoU.

SCOTUS issued temporary administrative stay on May 4 restoring telehealth access to mifepristone. Ongoing healthcare battleground with significant electoral implications for 2026 midterms. Combines with cost-of-living and Iran war as top driver of Democratic generic ballot advantage.

Trump cancelled strikes on June 11 after calling talks 'approved at highest levels.' ABCP reactive-agent model: probability of return to active hostilities within 30 days if MoU fails assessed at 0.09 (Tier III). Trump's strike cancellation consistent with reactive-agent framework — external pressure (deal optimism, oil prices) as primary driver rather than deliberate strategy.
